meta.title: Besøksstyringssystem — System Walkthrough
meta.subtitle: Step-by-step demonstration of the visitor lifecycle
meta.author: VMS Project Team

slides.title: Besøksstyringssystem
slides.subtitle: Visitor Management System — System Walkthrough
slides.notes: This presentation demonstrates the complete visitor management system through realistic scenarios. We follow six visitors through the system — from a smooth visit to a security denial — showing how each role interacts with the system at every step.

slides.title: Cast of Characters
slides.description: Six visitors, three system roles — each storyline shows a different path through the system.
slides.notes: The six visitors represent realistic scenarios: a routine external visit (Anna), a foreign contractor requiring security review (Thomas), an unannounced walk-in delivery (Petter), a denied visitor with verification failures (Ivan), a smooth in-house employee visit (Marte), and a pending request still awaiting sponsor action (Fatima).

slides.title: The Problem: Why a New Visitor Management System?
slides.description: Defense installations handle hundreds of visitors weekly — external contractors, foreign nationals, walk-in deliveries, and cross-site employees. Today's process is fragmented across disconnected systems, paper logs, and manual badge management. The result: slow onboarding, inconsistent identity checks, limited audit trails, and no centralized visibility.
bullets: Manual, paper-based visitor protocols that don't scale across sites
No unified identity verification — each site operates independently
Limited audit trail for security incidents and compliance (NSM, NIST CSF 2.0)
Badge management tied to physical presence — no automated activation/deactivation
Air-gapped RESTRICTED infrastructure adds complexity to every integration
Norwegian regulatory requirements: Sikkerhetsloven, GDPR, NSM guidelines
slides.notes: The core challenge is building a system that spans an air gap — external visitors register on UNCLASSIFIED, but all access decisions must be made on RESTRICTED. Current paper-based processes can't provide the audit trail required by Sikkerhetsloven or the automation needed to scale across multiple sites.

slides.title: Technological Environment
slides.description: The system operates across two classification levels separated by data diodes. Each side has its own infrastructure, databases, and authentication. The mock environment replicates this on a single machine using Kubernetes namespaces.
bullets: UNCLASSIFIED: Visitor Portal, Sponsor App, ID-porten auth, Brønnøysund lookups
RESTRICTED: Guard UI, Security UI, OnGuard 8.x, FREG, NKR, SAP HR
Data Diode: Bidirectional XML over NATS (2 unidirectional channels)
Auth: Mil Feide (defense OIDC) + ID-porten, both via Keycloak
Stack: TypeScript end-to-end — React frontends, Convex reactive backend
Infrastructure: On-prem K3s Kubernetes, PostgreSQL, Splunk, MIFARE DESFire EV3
slides.notes: Key constraint: no direct RESTRICTED-to-RESTRICTED paths between sites. Cross-site messages route: Site A RESTRICTED → Diode → Unclass WAN → Diode → Site B RESTRICTED. Each site must operate independently if WAN fails.

slides.title: Approach: Three-Layer Verification Model
slides.description: We solve this with a three-layer model: identity verification, approval workflows, and physical access control. The RESTRICTED side is the sole authority for all access decisions. An identity scoring model (100-point scale, Australian-style) provides quantitative risk assessment at every stage.
bullets: Layer 1 — Identity: Pre-registration (ID-porten), deep verification (FREG, NKR), 100-point scoring
Layer 2 — Approval: Sponsor approval + security officer review, batch and ad-hoc paths
Layer 3 — Physical: DESFire EV3 multi-app badges, time-bounded access, automated escort tracking
Diode-agnostic messaging: Abstracted gateway supporting multiple diode vendors per site
Design for accreditation: Security review ready from day one, continuous pentest cadence
Mock-first development: Full lifecycle demo on single machine validates architecture
slides.notes: The identity scoring model assigns points per verification source: ID-porten (40), passport (25), Mil Feide (40), FIDO2 (20), email (10), phone (5). Threshold is 60 points. Foreign nationals without Norwegian eID must compensate with additional documentation.

slides.title: Project Plan & Timeline

phases.name: Core Services
phases.label: Diode gateway, Convex backends, state machine

deliverables: Week 2: Kubernetes namespaces + network policies + Keycloak realms operational
Week 3: All mock APIs responding, test data seeded, diode delay proxy working
Week 5: End-to-end message flow through diodes, state machine verified
Week 7: All 5 UIs functional, full visitor lifecycle demonstrable
Week 10: Production integrations validated, pentest completed
slides.notes: Phase 0 (Foundation + Mock APIs) validates critical assumptions before committing to full build. Key risks: OnGuard API surface may be insufficient (Phase 0 spike), DESFire multi-app compatibility (early prototyping), diode vendor heterogeneity across sites (abstraction layer). Build order is designed so each phase produces a demonstrable increment.

slides.title: Visitor Lifecycle — Process Flow (BPMN 2.0)
slides.notes: BPMN 2.0 process flow showing the standard visitor lifecycle. The gateway at the Security Officer lane represents the identity score threshold check — visitors scoring below 60 are routed through manual review. All state transitions are logged for audit compliance.

slides.title: System Architecture Overview
slides.description: The system operates across an air gap. External visitors and sponsors interact via UNCLASSIFIED services. All access decisions are made on the RESTRICTED side. Data diodes carry XML messages between the two sides.
bullets: UNCLASSIFIED: Portal, Sponsor App, Contractor Admin
RESTRICTED: Guard UI, Security UI, OnGuard, FREG, NKR
Bidirectional XML data diodes (NATS messaging)
Keycloak authentication (ID-porten + Mil Feide)

annotations.text: 9 services monitored across both network segments

slides.title: Step 1 — Visitor Opens the Portal
slides.description: Anna Lindqvist from Kongsberg Defence navigates to the visitor portal. She needs to attend a technical review meeting for Project PEGASUS at SITE-A. The portal greets her in Norwegian with an ID-porten login button.

annotations.text: Anna sees the bilingual landing page
She authenticates via BankID through ID-porten

slides.title: Step 2 — Visit Dashboard
slides.description: After logging in, Anna sees her personal dashboard. All her visit requests are listed with status indicators. She can see her visit to SITE-A for Project PEGASUS is already 'Godkjent' (approved) alongside other visitors' requests.

annotations.text: All 5 visitor requests visible with status badges
Anna's visit is approved; Fatima's is still pending

slides.title: Step 3 — Registration Wizard
slides.description: When Anna clicked '+ Nytt besøk', she entered a 5-step wizard. Step 1 pre-fills her name from ID-porten and asks for visit type, purpose, site, dates, and host. The wizard then guides her through identity scoring, company verification, and a safety course.

annotations.text: 5-step wizard with progress indicator
Name pre-filled from ID-porten authentication

slides.title: Identity Scoring Model — 100-Point System
slides.description: Adapted from Australia's 100-point identity check. Each verification source earns points in one of three categories. Slot-based deduplication prevents double-counting (e.g., FIDO2 and TOTP both fill the 'authenticator' slot — only the highest wins).

sections.heading: Category A — Government / Federation
items: Mil Feide (defense federation): 50 pts
ID-porten / BankID: 40 pts

sections.heading: Category B — Physical / Biometric
items: Valid passport: 35 pts
In-person guard verification: 30 pts

sections.heading: Category C — Possession / Knowledge
items: FIDO2 hardware key: 20 pts (slot: authenticator)
TOTP app: 15 pts (slot: authenticator)
SMS OTP: 10 pts
Email verification: 5 pts

sections.heading: Scoring Pipeline (3 Stages)
items: Stage 1: Portal computes baseScore from identity sources
Stage 2: RESTRICTED recalculates + applies register modifiers
Stage 3: Resolve highest qualifying access tier via hardGates

justification: 100-point scale: Provides granular risk differentiation vs. binary pass/fail — enables tiered access instead of all-or-nothing
Category diversity: Requires sources from ≥2 categories, preventing single-vector identity fraud
Slot deduplication: Prevents inflating score by stacking similar authenticators (e.g., 3 TOTP apps)
Dual computation: Portal score is untrusted — RESTRICTED recalculates independently; divergence >10pts triggers automatic flag
Australian model: Proven at national scale, well-documented, adaptable to Norwegian defense context
slides.notes: The identity scoring model is implemented in packages/shared/src/identity-scoring.ts and shared between portal and restricted backends. Max achievable score is 190 (all sources + all positive register modifiers), but typical high-trust visitors score 85-110.

slides.title: Step 4 — Crossing the Air Gap
slides.description: Anna's visit request is submitted and queued for the data diode. The diode gateway wraps it in an XML envelope with a SHA-256 checksum and publishes it to NATS. After a 2-second simulated diode delay, the restricted-side gateway receives the message and creates a visit record for verification.
bullets: XML envelope with SHA-256 integrity checksum
NATS pub/sub between gateway pairs
2s delay ± 1s jitter simulating physical diode
At-least-once delivery with idempotency checks

slides.title: Diode Message Architecture
slides.description: Messages cross the air gap via an outbox/inbox pattern. Each side has a diodeOutbox table (pending messages) and diodeInbox table (received messages). The gateway polls outbox, wraps in XML, publishes to NATS. The receiving gateway writes to inbox for processing.

sections.heading: Message Envelope
items: XML wrapper with messageType, correlationId, SHA-256 checksum
Payload: JSON-serialized visit request or status update
Correlation ID links messages across the air gap bidirectionally

sections.heading: Outbox Table Schema
items: messageType (string), correlationId (string), payload (string/JSON)
status: pending → sent → failed
attempts (number), lastAttempt (timestamp)
Index: by_status — gateway polls for status='pending'

sections.heading: Delivery Guarantees
items: At-least-once delivery with idempotency via correlationId
Inbox deduplicates by correlationId index
Failed sends retry with exponential backoff (max 3 attempts)
Delay proxy: 2s ± 1s jitter simulating physical diode latency

justification: Outbox pattern: Survives process crashes — messages persist in Convex DB until confirmed sent. No data loss on restart.
XML envelopes: Required by most physical diode vendors (Advenica, Nexor, OPSWAT). JSON payload inside XML wrapper gives us both compatibility and developer ergonomics.
NATS transport: Lightweight pub/sub with no broker state — fits the constrained diode proxy model. Each direction is a separate NATS subject.
Diode-agnostic design: Each site may use a different diode product. The gateway abstraction means only the transport layer changes, not the application logic.
Correlation IDs: Enable bidirectional message tracking across the air gap without shared state between networks.
slides.notes: The diode gateway (packages/diode-gateway) polls every 500ms. The delay proxy (packages/diode-delay-proxy) subscribes to NATS and republishes with configurable delay. In production, these are replaced by actual diode hardware interfaces.

slides.title: Step 5 — RESTRICTED-Side Verification
slides.description: On the RESTRICTED network, Anna's request triggers automatic verification against three authoritative registers in parallel. Her identity score is 85/100 (ID-porten + passport + email), well above the 60-point threshold. FREG confirms she exists and is alive. NKR shows no clearance issues. She passes all checks.
bullets: FREG: Anna Lindqvist — alive, resident in Norway
NKR: No security clearance required for this visit level
SAP HR: Not an employee (external visitor — expected)
Identity score: 85/100 — exceeds 60-point threshold

slides.title: Register Verification Pipeline
slides.description: On the RESTRICTED side, each visit triggers parallel checks against 4 authoritative registers. Each register returns a result that maps to a score modifier. Blocks (deceased, emigrated) halt processing immediately. The verified score = baseScore + sum of all modifiers.

sections.heading: FREG (Folkeregisteret)
items: found_alive: +15 pts
not_found: -20 pts
deceased: BLOCK (score unchanged, visit halted)
emigrated: BLOCK (score unchanged, visit halted)

sections.heading: NKR (Klareringsregisteret)
items: active_clearance: +20 pts
no_clearance: 0 pts (neutral)
revoked: -50 pts (immediate suspension if on-site)

sections.heading: Brønnøysundregistrene
items: active: +10 pts
dissolved: -15 pts
company_not_found: -10 pts

sections.heading: SAP HR
items: employee: +10 pts
not_employee: 0 pts (neutral for external visitors)

justification: Parallel execution: All 4 registers checked simultaneously — reduces verification latency from ~4s sequential to ~1s parallel.
Modifiers not replacement: Register results adjust the base score rather than overriding it — preserves the value of strong initial identity verification.
Block semantics: Deceased/emigrated are absolute blockers regardless of score — no amount of other evidence can override a FREG block.
Divergence detection: If RESTRICTED verifiedScore differs from portal baseScore by >10 points, scoreDivergent flag triggers automatic security review.
Structured results: Each register result stored with register name, result type, modifier, and block flag — enables audit trail and re-scoring.
slides.notes: Register verification is implemented in packages/convex-restricted/convex/verification.ts. Mock registers respond from packages/mocks/registers/src/server.ts. The registerResults array on the visits table stores the full audit trail of all modifier applications.

slides.title: Step 6 — Sponsor Approves the Visit
slides.description: Maj. Ola Hansen logs into the Sponsor Portal via Mil Feide. His dashboard shows 5 active visits, 2 pending approvals, and 3 visits today. He sees Anna's request, assigns Lt. Kari Solberg as escort, and approves. The banner alerts him to 2 visits awaiting approval.

annotations.text: 5 active visits, 2 pending approval, 3 today at SITE-A
Alert banner: '2 besøk venter på godkjenning'
All visitors listed with type and status badges

slides.title: Step 7 — Guard Station: Anna Arrives
slides.description: Korp. Petter Olsen at the Guard Station sees today's expected visitors. Petter Svendsen (walk-in) and Marte Haugen (in-house) are already checked in. The guard verifies Anna's identity, confirms her escort, and checks her in. Tab badges show 2 on-site.

annotations.text: Tab badges show 2 visitors today, 2 on-site
Petter (walk-in) and Marte (in-house) already checked in

slides.title: Step 8 — Badge Issuance & On-Site
slides.description: The guard issues Anna a DESFire EV3 badge through the OnGuard integration. The system creates a visitor record, assigns a badge, and configures time-bounded access levels. The 'På stedet / On-Site' tab shows all active visitors with their escort assignments.

annotations.text: On-Site tab shows all currently active visitors
Each visitor has escort and check-in time visible

slides.title: Access Tier Resolution & Badge Lifecycle
slides.description: After verification, the system resolves the highest qualifying access tier by checking verifiedScore against tier thresholds AND hard gate conditions. Tiers are evaluated highest-first — the visitor gets the best tier they qualify for.

sections.heading: Access Tiers (score + hard gates)
items: escorted_day (≥40): FREG must not be negative
escorted_recurring (≥50): FREG must be positive (found_alive)
unescorted (≥70): FREG positive + NKR no flags
high_security (≥90): FREG positive + NKR active clearance
long_term_contractor (≥100): FREG positive + NKR no flags + Brønnøysund valid

sections.heading: Badge State Machine
items: pending → issued → active → deactivated → collected
OnGuard integration: badge key, visitor ID, badge number
Time-bounded activation: activateAt / deactivateAt timestamps
Access level IDs assigned based on resolved tier

sections.heading: DESFire EV3 Multi-App Model
items: One card per person, multiple site-specific applications
Delegated PICC master key: each site manages its own app independently
Card survives inter-site travel — no re-issuance needed

justification: Tiered access: Defense-in-depth — even low-trust visitors get escorted access rather than outright denial, enabling deliveries and short visits.
Hard gates: Score alone isn't sufficient — a contractor with score 100 but revoked clearance must not get unescorted access. Hard gates enforce categorical requirements.
Highest-first evaluation: Resolving from top tier downward means visitors automatically get the best access they qualify for, without manual tier selection.
DESFire multi-app: Each site operates independently — if Site A revokes a card app, Site B's app remains unaffected. No central card management dependency.
Badge lifecycle: Separate from visit lifecycle — badges can be pre-issued before arrival and auto-deactivated on check-out or timeout.
slides.notes: Access tier resolution is in packages/shared/src/identity-scoring.ts (resolveAccessTier). Badge management integrates with OnGuard via packages/convex-restricted/convex/visits.ts. The DESFire multi-app architecture is detailed in docs/plan/04-access-control.md.

slides.title: Step 9 — Check-Out & Badge Deactivation
slides.description: After her meeting, Anna returns to reception. The guard processes check-out — the badge is automatically deactivated via the OnGuard API. An audit log records the complete visit. Anna's story is complete: a smooth, end-to-end visitor lifecycle.

annotations.text: Check-Out tab for processing departing visitors
Badge deactivated automatically on check-out

slides.title: Story B — The Flagged Visitor
slides.description: Thomas Müller from Rheinmetall Defence (Germany) needs to attend a weapons integration test at SITE-A. His identity score is only 45/100 — below the 60-point threshold. He has a passport but no Norwegian eID and no clearance record in NKR.

slides.title: Step 10 — Security Officer: Flagged Visit
slides.description: Kapt. Eva Berg sees Thomas Müller in her Approval Queue with 'FLAGGET' status. His identity score of 45 is highlighted in orange — below threshold. She can see his company (Rheinmetall), purpose, sponsor (Oblt. Per Dahl), and the flagging reason.

annotations.text: 1 pending in queue, 1 active alert
Thomas Müller: score 45, FLAGGET, priority Medium
Full details: company, purpose, sponsor visible

slides.title: Story C — The Walk-In
slides.description: Petter Svendsen from NorLog Levering AS arrives unannounced at SITE-A reception with a delivery of spare parts (PO-2026-0412). He has no pre-registration. The guard contacts Sgt. Erik Nordby by phone for verbal approval and registers Petter directly.
slides.outcome: WALK-IN: CHECKED IN WITH ESCORTED-ONLY ACCESS

slides.title: Story D — The Denied Visitor
slides.description: Ivan Petrov from Unknown Technologies LLC submits a visit request for a 'business proposition'. His identity score is only 30. FREG cannot find him. Brønnøysundregistrene returns no match for his company. Kapt. Eva Berg reviews and denies the visit.
slides.outcome: DENIED — Identity unverifiable, company does not exist

slides.title: Step 11 — Audit Trail: All Stories Recorded
slides.description: Every action across all storylines is recorded in the Security Officer's Audit Trail. Diode messages, state transitions, verification results, and badge operations form a complete forensic record for NSM and NIST CSF 2.0 compliance.

annotations.text: Complete audit trail accessible to security officers
All diode messages and state transitions logged

slides.title: Step 12 — Operations Dashboard
slides.description: The management dashboard shows a bird's-eye view across both network segments. With all stories running: 9 services (8 up, 1 down), 2ms average response time, and diode message flow in both directions.

annotations.text: 8 of 9 services healthy, real-time latency monitoring
Diode message pipeline visible at bottom

slides.title: Summary: Five Storylines, Hardened Foundation
bullets: Story A–E: Complete visitor lifecycle across 5 scenarios and cross-site access
Tamper-evident audit log with SHA-256 hash chain and Splunk shipping
ABAC authorization: 9 roles × 6 actions, site-scoped, fully tested
DISA STIG baseline: 46 controls assessed, 8 passed, security pipeline active
121 backend tests passing (audit, visits, ABAC, identity scoring)
Security hardening roadmap: 9 CAT I fixes → 7 CAT II fixes → GDPR compliance
Mock infrastructure enables end-to-end demonstration on a single machine
Next: JWT auth, NATS TLS, diode HMAC signing, container hardening
slides.notes: Updated 2026-03-13. The system now includes tamper-evident audit logging, ABAC authorization, and a DISA STIG compliance baseline. Security hardening (E16–E18) is the next priority before accreditation review.

slides.title: Story E — Cross-Site Access
slides.description: Marte Haugen has permanent access at both Site A and Site B. Anna Lindqvist needs occasional time-bounded access to Site B. Thomas Müller requests Site B access but is denied due to his flagged status. One card, multiple sites — the DESFire multi-app model in action.
slides.outcome: ONE CARD, MULTIPLE SITES — CROSS-SITE ACCESS MANAGEMENT

slides.title: Step 13 — Marte: Permanent Dual-Site Access
slides.description: Marte Haugen (FD internal) arrives at Site B. Her DESFire card already has App 01 (Site A) and App 02 (Site B) encoded permanently. She taps the reader at Site B's entrance — the reader finds App 02, checks OnGuard access levels, and grants entry. No re-encoding needed.

annotations.text: Card has both App 01 and App 02 — permanent
Site B reader reads App 02 only, ignores App 01

slides.title: Step 14 — Anna: Time-Bounded Site B Visit
slides.description: Anna Lindqvist is approved for an occasional visit to Site B. At arrival, the Site B guard encodes App 02 onto her existing card (which already has App 01 from Site A). The app is time-bounded: 08:00-18:00 today. Anna can now tap into any standard area at Site B.

annotations.text: App 02 written onto existing card at Site B reception
Time-bounded: activates 08:00, deactivates 18:00

slides.title: Step 15 — Thomas: Site B Access Denied
slides.description: Thomas Müller requests access to Site B for a follow-up integration test. The request crosses the diode to Site B's RESTRICTED side. Verification fails: his flagged status from Site A is visible, identity score remains 45 (below threshold), and NKR shows no clearance. Site B denies the request — no App 02 is ever written to his card.

annotations.text: Flagged status from Site A propagates via diode
Score 45 < threshold 60 — denied at Site B

slides.title: Step 16 — Marte: Classified Zone Authorization
slides.description: Marte needs access to the SCIF at Site B. Kapt. Eva Berg verifies her NKR active clearance and approves classified zone access. The guard encodes App 10 (classified overlay) onto her card and reprints it with a red stripe and red holographic overlay. Marte's card now has App 01 + App 02 + App 10. The SCIF reader requires both App 02 AND App 10 (dual-app authentication).

annotations.text: App 10 encoded — separate key hierarchy from site apps
Card reprinted: red stripe + red hologram for manual fallback

slides.title: Step 17 — Anna: Site B Checkout
slides.description: Anna's meeting at Site B concludes. The guard processes checkout — App 02 is wiped from her card, OnGuard deactivates the badge. App 01 (Site A) remains untouched. Anna leaves with her card still valid for Site A. The audit trail records the complete cross-site visit lifecycle.

annotations.text: App 02 wiped — Site B access removed
App 01 unchanged — Site A access preserved

slides.title: DESFire EV3 Multi-App Card Layout
slides.description: One card holds multiple DESFire applications. Site apps (01, 02) handle standard access. Overlay apps (10, 11) handle special areas. Each app has independent AES-128 keys — compromising one never exposes another.

sections.heading: Site Applications (App 01, 02, ...)
items: One app per site, site-specific AES-128 keys
Reader reads only its own site's app
Permanent or time-bounded per access pattern

sections.heading: Overlay Applications
items: App 10: Classified zones (Central Security Authority keys)
App 11: High-value zones (Site Facility Manager keys)
Dual-app auth: site app + overlay required at special doors

sections.heading: Pool-Issued Cards
items: Blank DESFire cards from site pool for contractors, family, retired
Card accumulates apps across sites, wiped on return
Tracked: serial, issued-to, encoded apps, return date

justification: Site independence: each site manages own keys, no cross-site dependency
Cryptographic isolation: classified zone keys completely separate from standard access
DESFire EV3 supports 28 apps — 4-5 apps per card is well within limits
Pool cards follow same multi-app model as employee cards

slides.title: Visual Indicators — Manual Fallback Design
slides.description: When electronic access fails (power outage, emergency rush), guards need a visual way to verify special area authorization. A two-layer approach: color stripe for instant recognition, holographic overlay for forge resistance.

sections.heading: Color Stripe (instant, 2m recognition)
items: No stripe = standard access only
Red stripe = classified zone (App 10)
Blue stripe = high-value zone (App 11)

sections.heading: Holographic Overlay (forge-resistant)
items: Color-matched hologram confirms stripe authenticity
Requires inspection — used when there's time
Red+Blue dual hologram for both overlays (rare)

sections.heading: Operational Fallback
items: Rush: guard glances stripe, allows entry, logs manually
Power outage: stripe check + hologram inspection + photo/name
Card reprinted when overlay apps added after initial issue

justification: Stripe handles rush scenario — recognition at arm's length
Hologram handles skeptical-guard scenario — tamper-evident verification
1:1 mapping: stripe color = overlay app = zone type — no ambiguity
Combo scores highest on both instant-recognition AND forge-resistance

slides.title: Development Progress & Security Hardening
slides.subtitle: What's been built, what's planned, and how we get to accreditation
slides.notes: This section covers the implementation progress since the initial architecture demo, the security pipeline results, and the planned hardening roadmap.

slides.title: Development Progress — What's Been Built
slides.description: Since the initial architecture demo, the RESTRICTED backend has been hardened with tamper-evident audit logging, ABAC authorization, and a comprehensive test suite. The identity scoring engine is fully integrated across both classification sides.

sections.heading: E1 — Audit & Compliance Logging ✅
items: Tamper-evident SHA-256 hash chain: each entry links to previous via prevHash→hash
Audit hooks on all visit transitions, register verifications, and badge events
Query API with pagination, filtering by eventType/subjectId/time range
verifyChainIntegrity query detects tampered hashes and broken links
Splunk log-to-file shipping via cron-scheduled Convex action

sections.heading: E2 — Test Infrastructure (Partial)
items: convex-test harness: isolated Convex context per test (no running server needed)
21 audit log tests: chain integrity, field content, tamper detection, ABAC
14 visit tests: state transitions, invalid paths, audit logging, ABAC
100 identity scoring tests: all source combinations, register modifiers, edge cases
121 total backend tests passing

sections.heading: ABAC Authorization ✅
items: 9 roles: reception_guard, security_officer, escort, unit_manager, site_admin, auditor, sponsor, contractor_admin, external_visitor
6 protected actions: visit:approve, visit:admit, visit:checkout, audit:query, audit:verify, badge:issue
Site-scoped: actors only see/act on their own site's data
Shared policy engine in @vms/shared — used by restricted backend
parseActor helper validates actor identity on every mutation/query

justification: Audit-first: No defense system can pass accreditation without immutable audit trails — this was Sprint 1 priority
ABAC before JWT: Policy engine and enforcement points are in place; JWT integration (E16-F3) swaps the identity source without changing authorization logic
Test-driven: Every audit and ABAC feature was built with tests first — 121 tests prevent regressions
Hash chain: SHA-256 chain with OCC serialization prevents both tampering and race conditions
slides.notes: Key commits: 96830ad (audit schema), 3c46d89 (audit writer), 402d8bc–b97f015 (hooks), d2dc04e (ABAC engine), 50485ce (ABAC on visits). All work on the feat/e1-audit-logging and feat/abac-core branches, merged to main.

slides.title: DISA STIG Baseline Assessment
slides.description: A 5-skill automated security pipeline (static analysis, secret scan, SCA, container security, threat model) assessed 46 STIG controls. This establishes the security posture baseline and drives the hardening roadmap.

sections.heading: Pipeline Results — 2026-03-13
items: 46 controls tracked: 8 PASS, 17 FAIL, 9 MANUAL, 7 N/A, 5 NOT ASSESSED
24 CAT I findings across 5 tools — these block accreditation
Static analysis (semgrep): 11 audit calls without error handling
SCA (npm audit): 5 CVEs in hono / @hono/node-server
Container security: all 7 Dockerfiles run as root, no resource limits
Threat model (STRIDE+LINDDUN): 22 threats identified, 6 high-risk

sections.heading: Key Passes
items: Input validation: Convex typed validators + TypeScript strict mode
CSRF protection: Bearer token auth, OIDC state parameter
Command injection: No shell invocations in application code
SQL injection: Convex document DB — no SQL surface
Session uniqueness: Keycloak crypto-random session IDs
Audit record content: Full actor/action/target/timestamp structure

sections.heading: Tools Used
items: semgrep — STIG-mapped static analysis rules
gitleaks — secret scanning (0 findings in git history)
npm audit — dependency vulnerability scanning
trivy / dockerfile-lint — container security
STRIDE + LINDDUN — threat modeling (22 threats catalogued)

justification: Automated pipeline: Reproducible assessments on every branch — catches regressions before merge
STIG alignment: Norwegian defense context maps DISA STIGs to NSM/NIST CSF 2.0 controls
Risk-scored: Each finding has a computed risk score (impact × likelihood) — prioritizes work
Dev-only mitigations: Some findings (hardcoded creds, realistic PII) are classified as dev-only risk
slides.notes: Full baseline report at docs/compliance/reports/2026-03-13-full-pipeline.md. STIG status tracker at docs/compliance/stig-status.md. Pending tools: DAST and API fuzzing (require running servers).

slides.title: Security Hardening — CAT I (Accreditation Blockers)
slides.description: 9 features address all CAT I findings. These must be completed before any accreditation review. Sprint 1.5 priority. The highest-risk finding is client-supplied actor identity — fixable by wiring JWT auth from Keycloak.

sections.heading: Authentication & Identity (Risk: Critical)
items: E16-F3: Server-derived actor identity — replace client args with ctx.auth JWT (V-222425, risk 25)
E16-F4: Diode inbox access control — change to internalMutation or service auth (V-222425, risk 20)
E16-F8: Secure token storage — BFF pattern or sessionStorage + CSP (V-222577)

sections.heading: Transport & Data Integrity (Risk: High)
items: E16-F5: NATS TLS + NKey auth — encrypted diode transport (V-222543, risk 15)
E16-F6: HMAC-SHA256 signing on diode XML envelopes — tamper detection (V-222536, risk 15)
E16-F7: XXE protection — disable external entities in XML parser (V-222608)
E16-F9: crypto.randomUUID() for badge IDs — replace Math.random() (V-222536)

sections.heading: Operations & Dependencies (Risk: Medium-High)
items: E16-F1: Audit log error handling — fail-closed on critical ops (V-222485, 11 locations)
E16-F2: Dependency CVE fix — update hono ≥4.12.7, @hono/node-server ≥1.19.10 (V-222551, 5 CVEs)

justification: JWT-first: E16-F3 is the single highest-risk finding (score 25). Closes the trust gap between ABAC policy enforcement and identity verification.
HMAC before XML-DSig: HMAC-SHA256 is a pragmatic first step; full XML-DSig can follow if required for interoperability.
Fail-closed audit: If audit logging fails, the parent mutation must abort — prevents unlogged security events.
Dependency hygiene: 5 CVEs in hono include auth bypass and prototype pollution — single npm update fixes all.
slides.notes: E16-F3 depends on E13-F1 (Keycloak realm config). Interim mitigation: validate a shared secret for system/diode calls in parseActor. Full STIG V-ID mapping in docs/compliance/stig-status.md.

slides.title: Security Hardening — CAT II + GDPR (Post-Accreditation)
slides.description: CAT II findings and GDPR/privacy requirements. Important for production readiness but not accreditation-blocking. Scheduled across Sprints 3 and 7.

sections.heading: E17 — Container & Network Hardening (Sprint 3)
items: E17-F1: Non-root containers — adduser + USER directive in all 7 Dockerfiles (V-222548)
E17-F2: Resource limits + health checks — CPU/mem limits, HEALTHCHECK instructions (V-222549)
E17-F3: Docker network segmentation — separate unclass-net, restricted-net, diode-net (V-222545)
E17-F4: ABAC on read queries — protect listBySiteAndStatus, getVisitDetail (V-222425 ext)
E17-F5: Diode inbox schema validation — Zod/Convex validators per message type (V-222578)
E17-F6: Register error PII sanitization — never log raw fødselsnummer (V-222602)
E17-F7: Hardcoded credentials → .env files + K8s Secrets (V-222642)

sections.heading: E18 — GDPR / Privacy Compliance (Sprint 7)
items: E18-F1: Visitor portal privacy notice — GDPR Art. 13/14, bilingual (TM-021)
E18-F2: Data retention + purge — 24mo pseudonymize, 5yr delete, audit 5yr (TM-022)
E18-F3: Data Subject Access Request export — structured JSON per visitor (TM-022)
E18-F4: Cross-classification correlation minimization — one-time tokens (TM-018)

sections.heading: Key Metrics
items: CAT II findings: 38 across containers, 16 across Dockerfiles, 11 network
LINDDUN threats: 4 privacy-specific findings requiring GDPR remediation
Total features: 11 (7 CAT II + 4 GDPR)
Estimated sprints: 2 (Sprint 3 for CAT II, Sprint 7 for GDPR)

justification: Container hardening: Running as root in defense containers is unacceptable for production — non-root + read-only FS is baseline
Network segmentation: Docker default bridge allows cross-classification traffic — must mirror the physical air gap in the dev environment
GDPR: Sikkerhetsloven + GDPR requires both security AND privacy. Data retention and DSAR are legal obligations, not optional features
PII sanitization: Register errors currently may log fødselsnummer — violates both GDPR and NSM data minimization guidance
slides.notes: E17 runs in parallel with E6 (Diode Message Completeness) and E7 (Visitor Type Completeness) in Sprint 3. E18 is scheduled late (Sprint 7) because privacy controls build on top of completed auth (E13) and audit (E1) infrastructure.

slides.title: Updated Roadmap — 18 Epics, 74 Features

phases.name: Sprint 3
phases.label: Diode messages + visitor types + CAT II

phases.name: Sprint 6–7
phases.label: Missing UIs, auth federation, GDPR, NAR

deliverables: Week 2: ✅ Tamper-evident audit log, ABAC authorization, 121 tests passing
Week 3: JWT auth, NATS TLS, HMAC envelopes, XXE hardened, CVEs fixed
Week 5: Escort management, notifications, day-of re-verification operational
Week 7: All diode message types, visitor type routing, CAT II container hardening
Week 15: All 5+ UIs, Keycloak federation, GDPR compliance, NAR integration
slides.notes: Updated 2026-03-13. Sprint 1 complete. Sprint 1.5 (CAT I hardening) is the immediate next priority — these 9 features block accreditation. The backlog now tracks 18 epics and 74 features across 7+ sprints.

Audit chain implemented ✅ — error handling needed (E16-F1, 11 locations)

slides.title: Part 2: Technical Reference
slides.subtitle: Requirements, Data Models, State Machines, Design Decisions
slides.notes: The following appendix slides provide technical depth for developers and architects. Each section references back to the scenario steps where the concepts were demonstrated.

slides.title: Capability Requirements — Identity & Access

requirements.capability: All visitors must authenticate via ID-porten (external) or Mil Feide (internal) before registration
Identity score must be computed from ≥1 verified source across ≥2 categories
RESTRICTED side must independently recalculate identity score (no trust of portal score)
Score divergence >10 pts between portal and RESTRICTED should trigger automatic flag
All access decisions must be made exclusively on the RESTRICTED side
Access tiers must enforce both score thresholds AND hard gate conditions
Walk-in visitors should receive escorted_day access with guard-initiated registration
Visitors failing all register checks must be denied with audit record
Revoked NKR clearance should trigger immediate on-site suspension
Register checks (FREG, NKR, Brønnøysund, SAP HR) must run in parallel
Deceased/emigrated FREG results must block visit regardless of score
Foreign nationals without Norwegian eID may compensate with additional documentation

slides.notes: These capability requirements are derived from the system architecture documents (docs/plan/03-identity-verification.md and 04-access-control.md) and validated against the demonstrated scenarios.

slides.title: Capability Requirements — Diode, Physical & Audit

requirements.capability: Messages must cross air gap via outbox/inbox pattern with at-least-once delivery
All diode messages must carry SHA-256 integrity checksums
Gateway must be diode-vendor agnostic (support multiple products per site)
Failed sends should retry with exponential backoff (max 3 attempts)
Badges must be time-bounded with automatic deactivation on check-out or timeout
DESFire multi-app model must allow independent site-level card management
Escort assignments must track acceptance, delegation, and timeout states
All state transitions, diode messages, and verification results must be logged
Audit trail must satisfy NSM and NIST CSF 2.0 requirements
No fødselsnummer or clearance data may be stored on UNCLASSIFIED side
Separate Splunk instances per classification level for log segregation

slides.notes: Physical security requirements reference docs/plan/04-access-control.md. Audit requirements reference docs/plan/08-audit-compliance.md. Data minimization is a core GDPR compliance measure.

slides.title: Data Model — RESTRICTED: visits (Core Entity)
slides.notes: The visits table is the core entity on RESTRICTED — split here into two logical groups for readability. registerResults stores [{register, result, modifier, block?}] enabling re-scoring and forensic review. Four indexes support common query patterns: by status, by site+date, by site+status, and by diode correlation ID.

slides.title: Data Model — RESTRICTED: Supporting Tables
summary: diodeInbox: messageType, correlationId, payload, processedAt — receives from UNCLASSIFIED (idx: by_correlation, by_processed)
diodeOutbox: messageType, correlationId, payload, status, attempts — sends to UNCLASSIFIED (idx: by_status)
accessLevels: onguardId, name, zones[], requiredScoreTier — synced from OnGuard (idx: by_name)
slides.notes: Escort status enum: assigned → notified → accepted/delegated/declined/timed_out. Badge status enum: pending → issued → active → deactivated → collected. All infrastructure tables follow the same outbox/inbox pattern on both sides.

slides.title: Data Model — UNCLASSIFIED Side
summary: companyCache: orgNumber, name, organizationType, status, fetchedAt — cached Brønnøysund lookups (index: by_org)
courseCompletions: visitorId, completedAt — tracks security course completion for repeat visitors (index: by_visitor)
diodeOutbox/diodeInbox: same outbox/inbox pattern as RESTRICTED side — handles bidirectional message flow
slides.notes: Critical data minimization: NO fødselsnummer, NO clearance data, NO FREG results on the UNCLASSIFIED side. The identityScore is computed from portal-available sources only. The RESTRICTED side recalculates independently and never trusts this value.

slides.title: State Machines — Visit, Escort & Badge Lifecycles

branches.label: denied (terminal) — branches from flagged_for_review or verifying
cancelled (terminal) — can branch from any pre-arrival state

slides.notes: The visit lifecycle is the primary state machine managed by convex-restricted/convex/visits.ts. Key transitions: received→verifying triggers register checks, flagged_for_review requires manual security officer action, approved→day_of_check is a scheduled transition on visit date. Escort and badge machines run in parallel, linked by visitId.

slides.title: Design Decisions — Architecture & Security

decisions.options: A) Distributed decisions across both sides, B) RESTRICTED-only authority, C) UNCLASSIFIED pre-screening + RESTRICTED final
decisions.rationale: Eliminates attack surface on UNCLASSIFIED. If unclass side is compromised, attacker cannot approve visitors. Matches NSM guidance for classified environments.

decisions.options: A) Binary pass/fail, B) 3-tier (low/med/high), C) 100-point continuous scale
decisions.rationale: Enables 5 access tiers with different score thresholds. Modular — adding new identity sources doesn't require rethinking the model. Proven at national scale (Australia).

decisions.options: A) Full data on both sides, B) Minimal on unclass, C) No personal data on unclass
decisions.rationale: GDPR compliance + defense-in-depth. No fødselsnummer or clearance data on UNCLASSIFIED. If unclass DB is breached, exposure is limited to names, emails, and visit purposes.

decisions.options: A) Direct API calls through proxy, B) Shared message queue, C) Outbox table + polling gateway
decisions.rationale: Survives crashes (messages persist in DB). Auditable (every message logged). Works with any transport — NATS today, physical diode tomorrow. No shared state between networks.

decisions.options: A) Vendor-specific integration per site, B) Abstracted gateway interface
decisions.rationale: Each site may use different diode hardware (Advenica, Nexor, OPSWAT). Gateway abstraction means application logic is identical — only transport adapter changes per site.

slides.notes: These decisions are documented in detail in docs/plan/00-overview.md through 12-mock-infrastructure.md. Each was evaluated against NSM security requirements and NIST CSF 2.0 framework controls.

slides.title: Design Decisions — Technology & Operations

decisions.options: A) Single-app per card (one card per site), B) Multi-app per card (one card per person)
decisions.rationale: Visitors travelling between sites keep one card. Delegated PICC master key means each site manages its own app. No central card management dependency. Site independence maintained.

decisions.options: A) REST + polling, B) GraphQL subscriptions, C) Convex reactive queries
decisions.rationale: Real-time updates critical for guard station (new arrivals) and security officer (new flags). Convex provides reactive queries with zero boilerplate. TypeScript end-to-end with type-safe schema.

decisions.options: A) JSON only, B) XML envelope + JSON payload, C) Protocol Buffers
decisions.rationale: Most physical diode products expect XML. JSON payload inside gives developer ergonomics. SHA-256 checksum in XML header enables integrity verification at transport layer.

decisions.options: A) Build against production APIs, B) Mocks for all external systems, C) Partial mocks
decisions.rationale: Enables complete lifecycle demo on single machine. Validates architecture before production integration. Mocks double as test harness. Production APIs unavailable during initial development.

decisions.options: A) Polyglot (Python backend, TS frontend), B) TypeScript end-to-end, C) Go backend
decisions.rationale: Shared types between frontend and backend eliminate serialization bugs. Convex requires TypeScript. Single language reduces team cognitive load and enables shared packages.

slides.notes: Technology decisions prioritize developer velocity and correctness over raw performance. The defense context demands auditability and correctness; the mock-first approach de-risks integration before committing to production dependencies.

slides.title: Compliance Mapping — NSM, NIST CSF 2.0, GDPR

mappings.requirement: Physical access control with identity verification for classified areas
mappings.addressed: 100-point identity scoring + tiered access + DESFire badge + escort tracking

mappings.requirement: Visitor management with pre-registration, approval, and logging
mappings.addressed: Multi-step workflow: portal → sponsor → verification → guard → audit trail

mappings.requirement: Identities and credentials issued, managed, revoked for authorized devices/users
mappings.addressed: Badge lifecycle (issue → activate → deactivate → collect) via OnGuard integration

mappings.requirement: Physical access to assets managed and protected
mappings.addressed: Time-bounded badges, access level IDs, zone-based DESFire apps, escort requirements

mappings.requirement: Monitoring for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices
mappings.addressed: Real-time guard dashboard, on-site visitor tracking, automatic badge deactivation on timeout
Security officer UI: flagged visitors, divergent scores, revoked clearances → immediate alert

mappings.requirement: Data minimization — adequate, relevant, limited to purpose
mappings.addressed: No fødselsnummer on UNCLASSIFIED. No clearance data outside RESTRICTED. Minimal PII in portal.

mappings.requirement: Data protection by design and default
mappings.addressed: Classification-separated databases. Diode prevents data leakage. Outbox pattern provides audit trail.

mappings.requirement: Physical entry controls for secure areas
mappings.addressed: Guard check-in workflow, badge issuance, escort assignment, check-out with badge collection
Management dashboard: service health, diode message flow, visitor counts, on-site tracking

slides.notes: This compliance mapping covers the primary frameworks referenced in the system architecture. Full compliance assessment requires formal accreditation review (planned for Week 10 per project timeline). ISO 27001 Annex A is used as a supplementary checklist per docs/plan/08-audit-compliance.md.
